Rationale: T4 and T3 are the same thyroid hormones the body produces. Hypothyroidism is typically treated with T4 only (levothyroxine) medication, as this is the primary thyroid hormone that is released from the gland itself, and lowers thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) levels in the blood, which is used to diagnose hypothyroidism. However, T4 must be converted by other tissues (mainly the liver) into the active thyroid hormone T3, which is the hormone that facilitates increased metabolism. It is a lack of T3 properly reaching cells that is the true cause of hypothyroidism. Thus, supplementing with T3 in addition to T4 can be advantageous to correct hypothyroidism, especially in situations where there is a tendency to convert T4 into the anti-metabolic reverse T3.
Dosage/Notes:
Thyroid hormones can take 2-4 weeks to stabilize in terms of its effects. An improvement in key symptoms, energy levels, body temperature and pulse should be noticed within this time. If not, the dose can be increased. 

Start with ½ tablet (37.5 mcg) Thyronorm and ½ tablet (10 mcg) Linorma. Work with your primary advisor on adjusting the dosage if necessary.

Ensure to track morning temperature as described during the take-home assessment. Once temperature reaches 97.8°F  or higher consistently, along with symptom improvement, the correct dosage is likely to have been found.
